name: Thales
overview: Thales is a French aerospace, defence and security group that builds the sensors and mission systems navies and air forces depend on — radar, sonar, electronic warfare and surveillance. Since 2024 it has pulled its AI work under one banner called cortAIx, a network of AI 'accelerator' sites that put machine learning inside those systems to spot, classify and track threats faster. It frames all of this around an ethics label it calls TrUE AI — AI that is Transparent, Understandable and Ethical.

established.label: Its ethics label for AI
established.plain: In 2019 Thales unveiled 'TrUE AI', a set of principles it applies to its AI: Transparent (users can see the data behind a conclusion), Understandable (the system can explain and justify its results) and Ethical (it follows laws and human-rights standards). Thales' own framing is that it is not Thales' job to decide what is ethical, but to build tools that let its customers apply ethical rules. It remains the brand Thales uses for trustworthy defence AI.
established.sourceName: Breaking Defense — Ethics at heart of Thales' AI strategy

established.label: Its AI factory for defence
established.plain: In April 2024 Thales launched cortAIx, an in-house 'accelerator' to push AI into its defence and critical systems. It has three parts: cortAIx Lab (a research lab at Saclay, France), cortAIx Factory (which industrialises AI tools and use cases) and cortAIx Sensors (the AI built into Thales radar, sonar, radio and optical sensors). The stated goal is faster data analysis and decision support for armed forces, designed for hard constraints like cyber-security and running on limited onboard computing.
established.sourceName: GovConExec — Thales launches cortAIx AI accelerator

established.claim: cortAIx Sensors — AI in radar and sonar
established.label: AI inside the sensors
established.plain: cortAIx Sensors covers the AI built into Thales' core defence hardware — sonar, radar, radio and optical/optronic sensors. The pitch is that AI helps these sensors detect, identify and classify objects of interest faster and with less work for the human operator. This is the part of Thales' AI that touches actual warfighting equipment rather than back-office software.
established.sourceName: GovConExec — Thales launches cortAIx AI accelerator

established.claim: Pathmaster AI mine-hunting for Singapore Navy
established.label: AI clearing sea mines
established.plain: In 2025 Thales (via ST Engineering) won a contract to supply its Pathmaster mine-countermeasures system to the Republic of Singapore Navy. The system uses autonomous surface vessels carrying towed synthetic-aperture sonar, and Thales says its cortAIx AI processes the sonar data up to four times faster than before, at around 99% classification accuracy, to detect and identify mines in real time. Pathmaster is already in service with the British Royal Navy and the French Navy.
established.sourceName: Thales — AI-powered mine countermeasures for Singapore Navy

established.label: Scale of its AI effort
established.plain: As of early 2026 Thales says it employs more than 800 AI and data specialists and has filed over 200 patents for AI in critical systems, which it describes as making it the leading such patent applicant in Europe. (At cortAIx's 2024 launch the figure quoted was about 600 AI experts plus around 100 AI PhD students, so the workforce has grown.) These are the group's own headline numbers for its AI investment.
established.sourceName: The Defense Post — Thales brings AI accelerator to Germany

inProgress.claim: Sonar 76Nano — AI sonar for underwater drones
inProgress.label: Shrinking flagship sonar for drones
inProgress.plain: Announced in December 2025, Sonar 76Nano is a miniaturised, AI-powered sonar that compresses Thales' flagship Sonar 2076 into a small, modular unit for unmanned underwater vehicles, seabed sensors and crewed ships. It is built for hunting submarines and protecting undersea infrastructure such as cables and pipelines. Thales says it was developed in about ten months.
inProgress.sourceName: The Defense Post — Thales' new sonar for the undersea fight

inProgress.claim: CURCO + Golden AI electronic warfare (PERSEUS awards)
inProgress.label: AI for jamming and radar detection
inProgress.plain: In November 2025 two Thales electronic-warfare innovations won French Navy PERSEUS awards. CURCO is a compact EW payload that fits on drones, ships or vehicles to detect enemy radar emissions and feed tactical data to operators in real time, with an optional onboard jammer planned. Golden AI is a tool that analyses intercepted radar signals up to four times faster and trains AI models to identify which radar produced a given emission — a step toward 'cognitive' electronic warfare.
inProgress.sourceName: ASD News — Thales wins two PERSEUS awards for EW and AI

inProgress.claim: Dassault Aviation partnership for combat-air AI
inProgress.label: AI for future air combat
inProgress.plain: In November 2025 Thales (through cortAIx) and Dassault Aviation signed a partnership to develop 'controlled and supervised' AI for defence aeronautics — including manned and unmanned aircraft, observation and situation analysis, and military planning and decision-making. Dassault framed it as work for the collaborative air combat of the future. Both sides say it will comply with national, European and AI Act ethical rules. It was announced publicly at an AI summit in Paris attended by President Macron.
inProgress.sourceName: Airforce Technology — Dassault Aviation and Thales cortAIx AI for aeronautics

inProgress.label: Expanding the AI network
inProgress.plain: In January 2026 Thales opened a cortAIx site in Germany, its fifth after France, the UK, Canada and Singapore. The German hub focuses on trustworthy military AI for cyber-defence, sensor fusion, command and control, and use cases like autonomous cyber-defence and agent-based penetration testing — AI meant to keep working under pressure and resist manipulation in hostile environments. It is part of a push toward 'sovereign' European AI capability.

latest.claim: Naval Group invests in cortAIx France
latest.label: A shipbuilder backs the AI hub
latest.plain: In February 2026 French naval shipbuilder Naval Group invested in cortAIx France to speed up AI integration in naval defence systems while keeping control of its own algorithms ('algorithmic sovereignty'). The two will work across collaborative combat, decision support, electronic warfare, training/simulation and logistics, and Naval Group experts from its Ollioules digital centre will join cortAIx teams. Naval Group called it 'a major acceleration for the future of engineering and naval systems'.
latest.sourceName: Naval Today — Naval Group backs Thales' cortAIx

suppliers.name: ST Engineering
suppliers.role: partner / prime — built the uncrewed surface vessels carrying Thales' Pathmaster AI mine-hunting system for the Singapore Navy
suppliers.sourceName: Thales — AI-powered mine countermeasures for Singapore Navy

suppliers.name: Dassault Aviation
suppliers.role: partner — joint development of supervised AI for future manned/unmanned air combat
suppliers.claim: Dassault's CTO Pascale Lohat said the work focuses on 'research and innovation programmes dedicated to the collaborative air combat of the future'.
suppliers.sourceName: Airforce Technology — Dassault Aviation and Thales cortAIx

suppliers.name: Naval Group
suppliers.role: partner / investor — invested in cortAIx France for AI in naval systems
suppliers.claim: Naval Group's CEO called it 'a major acceleration for the future of engineering and naval systems, in which AI plays an increasingly important role'.
suppliers.sourceName: Naval Today — Naval Group backs Thales' cortAIx

suppliers.name: Republic of Singapore Navy
suppliers.sourceName: Naval News — Thales to provide Pathmaster to Singapore

unknowns: Contract values for most of these deals (Pathmaster, the cortAIx partnerships) are not disclosed publicly, and headcount/patent figures are Thales' own self-reported numbers that cannot be independently verified. How well the AI performs operationally — the claimed '4x faster' and '99% classification accuracy' for Pathmaster sonar — comes from Thales rather than independent testing.
